abstract: |
  Simulation-based research on the future of social-ecological systems often focuses on exploratory scenarios that extrapolate current trends to identify potential changes in the state of system components. While such scenarios are useful for highlighting the consequences of continued anthropogenic environmental degradation, they provide little guidance for averting the undesirable futures they predict. In contrast, normative scenarios, which explicitly focus on defining positive outcomes for both nature and society, have the potential not only to provide solution-oriented results for decision-makers, but also to mobilise public support through the use of inclusive, participatory processes. Several frameworks have been developed to guide the co-creation of normative scenarios for socio-ecological systems, but examples of operationalising the qualitative narratives produced using these frameworks into inputs for quantitative simulation modelling are still limited.
  To contribute to this research gap, this paper presents an example of how aspects of normative scenarios can be realised within a model of land use and land cover change. This is achieved through a combination of data-driven approaches to encapsulate scenario-specific differences in local and global scale phenomena and iterative expert elicitation to quantify descriptive trends from narratives. This approach is demonstrated with a case study simulating five scenarios of landscape change (three normative and two exploratory) in Switzerland between 2020 and 2060. The results of this case study are evaluated in terms of their consistency with the outcomes expressed in the scenario narratives. However, this research also demonstrates the importance of sharing simulation results with stakeholders, not only to identify improvements in the modelling process, but also to encourage broader reflection on the desirability and plausibility of future scenarios.

keywords:
  - Scenario modelling
  - Land use change
  - Cellular Automata
  - Normative scenarios

# Introduction {#sec-introduction}

In recent decades, research envisioning future scenarios for the combined natural and human environments has been dominated by pessimistic, often dystopian, predictions [@hjerpe2009; @robbins2013; @bennett2016; @whyte2018]. While it is arguable that such negative outlooks are justified in the face of the increasing severity of global environmental degradation in the 21st century, at the same time they offer little input in terms of developing solutions to achieve more positive outcomes for nature and society [@mcphearson2016].

In response to this, there has been a growing recognition of the importance of positive scenarios that incorporate a plurality of normative goals for the future and attempt to encapsulate possibilities for transformative change [@nassauer2004; @bennett2016; @mcphearson2016; @luederitz2017; @iwaniec2020]. The rationale is that such positive scenarios not only offer explicit input for decision makers but if they are derived through an inclusive, participatory, process they have the potential to foster a wider public will to action [@mcphearson2016; @lavorel2019]. In order to mainstream the development of normative socio-ecological scenarios, several frameworks have been proposed such as the Sustainable Future Scenarios (SFS) framework [@iwaniec2020] as well as the Intergovernmental Science-Policy Platform on Biodiversity and Ecosystem Services Nature Futures Framework (IPBES-NFF: @pereira2020). The IPBES-NFF in particular has already been applied in several examples to produce scenario narratives and visions [@lembi2020; @kuiper2022; @duran2023; @mayer2023]. However, whilst qualitative scenario descriptions are a useful starting point, to progress towards the implementation of the changes they describe narratives should ideally be distilled into evidence-based goals and targets [@mcphearson2016].

In this regard, operationalizing scenarios through quantitative simulation modeling to explore the spatial-temporal impacts on critical aspects of the study system is often highly useful [@verburg2004; @verburg2006]. The goal of modeling in this context should not be to provide an absolute representation of the scenarios but rather it should be part of an iterative process of scenario refinement whereby the models and their outputs act as "boundary objects" [@castella2014; @verburg2019] to foster discussions with stakeholders. Two important aspects that simulation modeling can help elucidate are the coherency of scenarios [@volkery2008] as well as the balance between their perceived desirability and plausibility [@iwaniec2020]. This is of particular importance for normative scenarios which, by virtue of striving for desirable outcomes, often necessitate radical changes from the status quo and hence may be considered to lack plausibility as compared to exploratory scenarios that simply extrapolate current trends [@wiek2014]. Furthermore, simulation modeling is also important in the case of scenarios that would likely result in trade-offs that must be negotiated by society [@rounsevell2012; @mcphearson2016].

The modeling of land use and land cover change (LULCC) is a field in which there has been an extensive history of scenario simulation research, likely because of the long acknowledged contribution of anthropogenic LULCC as a driver of intersecting global environmental challenges [@ojima1994; @mahmood2014; @molotoks2021]. Nevertheless, within this domain, most studies have focused on exploratory scenarios with limited attention paid to the normativity of both their framing and outcomes [@verburg2019]. However, in recent years, there have been some attempts to move beyond exploratory scenarios with several studies [@murray-rust2013; @wolff2018; @wu2019; @schirpke2020; @peng2021] including scenarios framing environmentally positive outcomes although none of these were presented as explicitly normative (i.e they were not created through a participatory process involving discussion as to the desirability of the outcomes). Whereas @verkerk2018 do deliberately explore scenario normativity but here the authors evaluate normative visions against modeled scenarios from another source in an *ex post* fashion rather than modeling their own visions. Perhaps, the most comprehensive study engaging with normative scenario creation with subsequent quantitative LULCC modeling is that of @vannier2019, who demonstrated a participatory approach to integrate regional and local scale socio-economic, climate and ecological dynamics. The fact that there are so few examples of the application of co-constructed normative scenarios, given the increasing frequency of studies published over the last decade [@tong2020], shows that there is still a clear need within LULCC modeling to incorporate a greater diversity of perspectives beyond the focus on the extrapolation of current trends.

Within the broader field of LULCC modeling, Constrained Cellular Automata (CCA) are a class of models that have enjoyed enduring popularity for scenario simulation [@triantakonstantis2012; @aburas2016; @brown2022]. @thompson2020 ascribe this popularity to the fact that CCAs represent LULCC as a phenomenon rather than as a process, i.e. they abstract the actions of the actors involved, which can make them easier to explain to non-experts. In addition, they offer a relatively extensible framework, as demonstrated by the diverse features that have been incorporated in recent years such as the integration with other model types [@he2005], uncertainty quantification methods [@salap-ayça2018], and 3D dynamics [@qian2020]. At the highest level of abstraction LULCC-CCAs treat landscapes as a pixelated grid of cells of individual LULC states with cells changing state over time typically through the combination of two mechanisms: Firstly, the spatial distribution of potential LULCC is determined through the calculation of cellular probabilities of change to alternate land use land cover states, often referred to as transition potential (TP) [@white1997]. The method by which this is achieved differs between popular LULCC-CCAs, although it is commonly based on the quantification of correlative relationships with explanatory variables such as biophysical, socio-economic and neighborhood (focal) predictors using a statistical model [@tobler1979; @garcía-álvarez2018; @ren2019]. Secondly, the amount of cellular LULCCs that occur in a given simulation time step is prescribed by values of transition rates extrapolated from historic LULC data, with a model specific allocation algorithm selecting the required number of cells to change based upon their transition probabilities [@mas2018].

The two general mechanisms of LULCC-CCAs offer several opportunities to instantiate aspects of scenarios. Firstly, the data supplied to predict cellular TP values can be both specific to the different scenarios being modeled as well as dynamic over the simulation time steps [@liao2023]. For example, in scenarios contrasting differential levels of climatic change, the data provided may come from exogenous climatic models of different Relative Concentration Pathways (RCPs) [@song2019]. A second method is to alter predicted cellular TP values, this can be done directly: such as spatially designated land management policies, for example, reducing the potential for transition to urban LULC states inside environmental protection areas [@poelmans2010; @gharbia2016; @price2017; @clerici2019; @daneshi2021; @peng2021; @frank2023], or indirectly by altering the influence of variables within the statistical model used to calculate TPs [@troupin2016; @gago-silva2017]. Another means is to adjust parameters of the LULCC-CCA's allocation algorithm that may, for example, increase the creation of new patches of LULC class cover relative to the expansion of existing areas [@prieto-amparán2019]. Finally, scenarios can be instantiated through individual specification of the transitions rates determining the quantities of LULCC [@shrestha2018; @cheng2020; @schirpke2020; @peng2021; @kiziridis2023; @molinero-parejo2023].

Despite these multiple means of operationalizing different facets of scenarios in LULCC-CCAs, @brown2022 note that most studies typically explore only a small subset of scenario components. Possible reasons for this could be a lack of future projected data for the scenario drivers or that the scenarios themselves are not developed in such a manner as to incorporate enough detail for substantial exploration. This, in turn, is perhaps a contributing factor to explain the relative lack of normative scenarios in LULCC modeling as it is comparatively easier to specify exploratory scenarios that simply extrapolate existing trends.

With this in mind, the goal of this research is to present an approach to modeling normative, nature positive scenarios of land-use change using an LULCC-CCA model that substantively operationalizes scenario components including: Dynamic data to represent variations in future climatic, economic, and demographic trends; multi-region modeling of LULC transitions combining both statistical and process-based techniques; and mechanisms to realize the effect of spatial policies such as the expansion of nature conservation areas. We demonstrate this approach through a case study for the country of Switzerland by simulating five scenarios framing the development of a national Ecological Infrastructure between 2020-2060, recently elaborated by @mayer2023. We discuss the main trends that characterize the scenario simulations and notable differences between them. Finally, we illustrate the importance and challenges of instrumentalising the simulation results to prompt feedback from the stakeholders involved in the creation of the scenarios, thereby 'closing the loop' of the participatory process.

# Methods

## Overview of methodological approach

To perform scenario-based simulation of future LULCC in Switzerland between 2020 and 2060, this research employed three key processes illustrated in @fig-workflow: First, expert participation was used to translate scenario narratives into quantitative LULCC model inputs. The scenarios were then simulated using a comprehensive LULCC-CCA model created by combining the statistical programming software R (4.2.2)[@rcoreteam2022] with the environment modeling software Dinamica EGO (7.2.0)[@soares-filho2009]. In the interest of open science, the LULCC-CCA model (<https://anonymous.4open.science/r/LULCC-CH-660B/>) along with the research data (<https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.8263509>) have been made available alongside this manuscript. @fig-workflow includes a simplified overview of the processes taking place within the LULCC-CCA model, although a more expansive representation is provided by @fig-process. Subsequent sections summarize the details of the model processes, however, for brevity, some material has been presented as appendices. The final step was to instrumentalize the results of the simulation modeling, using an interactive web platform, as a means to gather expert and stakeholder feedback on their plausibility and coherency.

```{=tex}
\newpage
\blandscape
```
::: column-page
![Overview of the three stages of the approach used to model normative future scenarios of landscape change for Switzerland (Orange loops represent participatory processes, and blue loop simulation modelling)](figures_tables/graphical_abstract_landscape.png){#fig-workflow width="1200"}
:::

```{=tex}
\elandscape
\newpage
```
## Overview of scenarios

To address nationally declining biodiversity, in 2012, the federal government of Switzerland ratified the Swiss Biodiversity Strategy (SBS) \citepalias{federalofficefortheenvironment2012}. One of the goals of the SBS is the development of Switzerland's Ecological Infrastructure (EI) as a "network of protected and connected areas that contribute to safeguarding the essential services of ecosystems for society and economy ... and form the basis for a rich and resilient biodiversity" \citepalias{federalofficefortheenvironment2012}. To contribute towards the implementation of this goal, @mayer2023 defined five narrative scenarios to frame the development of EI in Switzerland between 2020 and 2060 alongside expected demographic, economic and climatic changes in Switzerland. The scenarios were produced through a participatory process with the aim of envisioning nature-positive outcomes using the IPBES Nature futures framework [@pereira2020; @duran2023]. As such, three of the scenarios are explicitly normative and encapsulate the development of EI from different perspectives of societal-nature relationships, which are reflected in their names: EI for Nature (EI-NAT); EI for Society (EI-SOC); EI as Culture (EI-CUL)[@mayer2023]. The remaining two scenarios are exploratory and are meant to serve as a contrast to the others, these are Business-as-Usual (BAU) and Growth and Extinction (GR-EX) which represents a worst case environmental scenario where existing trends in negative drivers are amplified and existing measures to mitigate negative environmental trends are rolled back. For the full narratives of the scenarios readers should refer to @mayer2023. We have provided however, statements related to the key themes in @tbl-Spat_ints.

## Land use land cover transition identification {#sec-trans_identification}

The process of identifying LULC transitions, i.e. pixel changes from an initial specific LULC class to a different final LULC class, was based upon a preceding study by @black_re-considering_2023. We utilized classified LULC datasets for four time periods from the Swiss area statistics \citepalias{sfso2021}: 1981-1985; 1992-1997; 2004-2009; 2013-2018. It is important to note that the date range for each dataset is the extent of the data-collection period, and each product actually represents a single LULC map. In this regard, for simplicity we will henceforth refer to the datasets by the latter year of the data-collection period only. The LULC data is originally presented as point data generated from aerial photography using a classification scheme that identifies 72 LULC classes \citepalias{fso2018}. However, for the purpose of modeling, all maps were converted to 100m resolution rasters. Given that transitions are identified between two time points, having four historic LULC maps meant that we had three LULC transition periods with which to calibrate our model: 1985-1997; 1997-2009; and 2009-2018, henceforth referred to as calibration periods. Following @black_re-considering_2023, we aggregated the original 72 LULC classes in the data to 10 classes: Alpine pastures; Closed forest; Glacier; Grassland; Intensive agriculture; Open Forest; Shrubland; Permanent crops; Urban/amenities; Static (see Appendix @sec-LULC_aggregation_app for a table of class aggregations).

Instances of LULC transitions were identified by overlaying the maps for each calibration period and extracting pixels according to changes from their initial LULC class (start map) to their final class (end map). With 10 aggregated classes, this gives the potential for 100 possible class-class transitions which would be unfeasible for modeling. Thus, as per @black_re-considering_2023, we filtered to viable LULC transitions for each period by first excluding illogical transitions (e.g. from static to other classes) as well as applying a threshold to remove transitions that led to a change in less than 0.5% of the total area of the initial class.

The instances of LULC transitions were then combined with instances of non-transitions (i.e. pixels with the corresponding initial class for a given transition but a non-corresponding final class), to give separate datasets for each transition. To better reflect the potential for regionalized differences in LULCC processes, the datasets were subdivided according to the six biogeographical regions of Switzerland [@gonseth2001]. Notably, not all transitions occurred in all regions (for example, because some regions contained no glacial area) or across all calibration periods. A table detailing the transitions identified for each of the calibration periods is included as Appendix @sec-transitions_app.

## Transition potential prediction {#sec-trans_model_method}

The method of creating the statistical models to be used to iteratively predict cellular TP within the LULCC-CCA was refined from that of @black_re-considering_2023. The predictors chosen for the modeling were grouped into two categories: Firstly, those related to land suitability and accessibility. Secondly, neighborhood predictors capturing the influence of localized LULC composition on the ocurrence of LULC transitions. The details of the suitability and accessibility predictors are provided in @tbl-preds, which also details which of the predictors were treated as static (i.e. unchanging) during simulations and which were dynamic (see section @sec-future_preds).

```{r}
#| label: tbl-preds
#| tbl-cap: Predictor data used in statistical models of transition potential.
#| tbl-colwidths: [45,45,10]
#load table
Preds <- read_xlsx(here("publication/figures_tables/Predictors.xlsx"))
#output as markdown table with Knitr::kable
knitr::kable(Preds)
```

The approach for the preparation of neighborhood predictors was also adopted from @black_re-considering_2023. In summary, 5 LULC classes were selected as active classes (i.e. perceived to influence LULC transitions) and a set of 25 Pythagorean matrices of varying size (9--121 cells) with randomized central values and decay rates were applied as focal moving windows over the LULC layers, masked for these classes, for each calibration period. This resulted in a total of 125 neighborhood predictor layers at 100m resolution for each period.

In order to increase the efficiency of TP prediction within the LULCC-CCA simulations, a two-step predictor variable selection approach was applied to derive parsimonious (i.e. compact and non-redundant) predictor sets for each regionalized transition dataset [@black_re-considering_2023]. This approach involved pairwise correlation-based filtering using univariate regression models followed by model-embedded filtering using the Guided Regularized Random Forests algorithm [@adde2023a; @black_re-considering_2023].

Following predictor variable selection, the Random Forests (RF) algorithm [@breiman2001] was used to create TP models for each regionalized transition dataset using the *randomForest* R package [@cutler2022]. The accuracy of the RF TP models was assessed independently of the CCA model, using hold-out validation (70:30 training and test set splits using proportional random sampling without replacement) with multiple performance metrics in a process that @black_re-considering_2023 refer to as stage 1 validation. This allowed for hyper-parameter tuning to derive the optimal RF specification, which was found to be 500 tree forests under the default classification settings for minimum size of terminal nodes and number of variables sampled at each split with tree-level proportional sampling used to mitigate class imbalance [@black_re-considering_2023]. The final RF TP models, re-trained using the full datasets, were incorporated in the LULCC-CCA with the corresponding TP model from each calibration period used for parameter calibration (section @sec-CA_calibration) and the models from the most recent period (2009-2018) used for future simulation steps (section @sec-scenario_operationalization).

The exception with regards to TP prediction was the transition from the glacier class to the static class. Instead of using an RF model, this transition was realized using spatially explicit predictions of future glacier evolution by @zekollari2019 (for details see Appendix @sec-glacier_app), which were implemented deterministically in each simulation step following the TP prediction and allocation of the other transitions.

## Cellular automata parameter calibration {#sec-CA_calibration}

Dinamica EGO uses a combination of two algorithms, Patcher and Expander, for allocating cellular LULC transitions, which represent the processes of the creation of new patches of LULC classes and the expansion of existing patches respectively [@soares-filho2009]. These algorithms require the calibration of 5 parameters (henceforth referred to as allocation parameters) for every transition: Mean patch size, Patch size variance, Patch isometry, and the relative % of transitions to be allocated by each algorithm (i.e % by patcher, % by expander). To calibrate the allocation parameters, we first used the historic LULC maps from the calibration periods to estimate parameter values using the R package *SDMtools* [@vanderwal2014], which were then refined using brute force simulation. This involved applying a random permutation (from normal distribution with 5% bounding) to each transition parameter value for 100 repetitions to produce candidate sets of allocation parameter values. These candidate sets were then tested using the Dinamica EGO model to simulate LULCC between 2010 and 2020 (i.e covering the final calibration period) using a 5-year time step. The results of these simulations were used to identify the candidate set of parameter values that displayed the greatest value of the minimum similarity score produced by Dinamica EGO's "fuzzy reciprocal similarity" map comparison method [@soares-filho2009; @soares-filho2013]. The calibrated set of allocation parameter values are presented in Appendix @sec-parameter_calibration_app along with summary statistics of the minimum similarity score across the candidate sets. These calibrated parameter values were used as the basis for all future simulations with some scenarios involving deliberate modifications of values (section @sec-manipulation).

## Scenario operationalization {#sec-scenario_operationalization}

@tbl-scenario_operationalisation provides a summary of differences in LULCC drivers between the five scenarios and how these were operationalised within the LULCC-CCA model. The three methods used to instantiate drivers were: temporally dynamic variants of variables used for cellular TP prediction (Dynamic predictors); mechanisms to manipulate TP values and allocation parameters (Spatial interventions); and transition rates to control the amount of cellular changes under each transition. The influence of these factors within the process of the LULCC-CCA model is visualised in @fig-process with details of each approach provided in the subsequent sections.

Given the computation required for separate regionalized TP prediction of `r length(unique(paste0(unlist(Trans_tbl_wide[Trans_tbl_wide$Period == "2009_2018", "From"]), "_", unlist(Trans_tbl_wide[Trans_tbl_wide$Period == "2009_2018", "To"]))))` LULC transitions as well as the calculation of dynamic predictors within each time step, the decision was made to use 5-year time steps to simulate LULCC for each of the five scenarios between 2020-2060. This resulted in eight simulated LULC maps for each scenario.

```{r, message=FALSE, warning = FALSE}
#| label: tbl-scenario_operationalisation
#| tbl-cap: Overview of Scenario drivers and the methods by which they were operationalized in the LULCC-CCA model.

library(kableExtra)
#stop kableExtra automatically setting the output format of all kable objects to 'html' which means that markdown syntax is not rendered correctly
options(kableExtra.auto_format = FALSE)

#load table
scenario_tbl <- as.data.frame(read_xlsx(here("publication/figures_tables/Scenario_operationalisation.xlsx")))

#identify indices of scenario columns by matching column names to Scenario_abbrevs$abbrev
scenario_cols_ind <- which(colnames(scenario_tbl) %in% Scenario_abbrevs$abbrev)

#replace '_' in scenario_names
colnames(scenario_tbl) <- str_replace_all(string = colnames(scenario_tbl), pattern = "_", "-")

#add footnote symbol "*" to scenario columns in row scenario_tbl$Driver == "Economic development"
scenario_tbl[scenario_tbl$Driver == "Economic development", scenario_cols_ind] <- (paste0(scenario_tbl[scenario_tbl$Driver == "Economic development", scenario_cols_ind], "*"))

#add footnote symbol "\u2020" to scenario columns in row scenario_tbl$Driver == "Climate change"
scenario_tbl[scenario_tbl$Driver == "Climate change", scenario_cols_ind] <- (paste0(scenario_tbl[scenario_tbl$Driver == "Climate change", scenario_cols_ind], "\u2020"))

#add footnote symbol "\u2021" to scenario columns in row scenario_tbl$Driver == "Population development"
scenario_tbl[scenario_tbl$Driver == "Population development", scenario_cols_ind] <- (paste0(scenario_tbl[scenario_tbl$Driver == "Population development", scenario_cols_ind], "\u2021"))

#output with KableExtra add a header above the scenario columns: "scenario trend overview"
scenario_tbl%>%
  kableExtra::kable(align=rep('c', ncol(scenario_tbl)-1),
      booktabs = T,
      format = "latex",
      longtable = T,
      escape = TRUE)%>%
  kable_styling(full_width = FALSE,
                latex_options = c("hold_position"),
                font_size = 8)%>%
  collapse_rows(columns = 1,
                valign = "top",
                latex_hline= "major",
                longtable_clean_cut = FALSE)%>%
  row_spec(0,bold=TRUE) %>%
  column_spec(column = 1, width = "10em")%>%
  column_spec(column = 2, width = "8em")%>%
  column_spec(column = 3:7, width = "9em")%>%
  footnote(symbol = c('Swiss future population growth scenarios 2020-2050 (SFSO 2020);',
                      'Future climate change under different Representative Concentration pathways (RCPs), adapting data from  Broennimann and Guisan 2023;',
                      'Future economic scenarios from Cretegny and Müller 2020'),
           threeparttable = TRUE,
           escape = FALSE,
           footnote_as_chunk = TRUE,
           symbol_title = "Symbols: ")%>%
landscape()
#threeparttable argument ensures footnote width corresponds to table width

```

### Dynamic predictors {#sec-future_preds}

Scenario specific dynamic predictor data was produced for the drivers of climate change, population development and economic development with the corresponding suitability and accessibility variables marked as 'dynamic' in @tbl-preds. The majority of the sources of data utilized for these drivers (see symbols note in @tbl-scenario_operationalisation) presented several of their own internal scenarios and as such it was necessary to match the source specific scenarios to our scenarios. These correspondences are presented as the entries under these drivers in @tbl-scenario_operationalisation. Additional data manipulation was required to align the future projected data sources with that of the equivalent historical variables, which is described in Appendix @sec-Sim_data_prep_app. Additionally, the focal LULC neighborhood predictors were also updated during each simulation time step, utilizing the same focal moving window approach as the historic versions (section @sec-trans_model_method) but instead used the simulated LULC map from the previous simulation time step as input.

### Transition rates {#sec-trans_rates_method}

The cellular transition allocation process in Dinamica EGO is constrained by input in the form of rates of transitions (the % of the initial class area to be converted) for each time step in the simulation. To establish transition rates for each of our five future scenarios, we devised an approach that iterated between quantitative estimation and expert elicitation, with the details of this presented in Appendix @sec-trans_rates_app.

### Spatial interventions in transition potential and allocation parameters {#sec-manipulation}

The spatial interventions were derived by extracting statements from the scenario narratives [@mayer2023], grouped according to drivers, that pertained to spatialized differences in the locations of LULCC processes. These statements were used to create eight corresponding spatial intervention mechanisms, which were modified across the scenarios to give a total of `r nrow(Spat_ints)` different interventions. As mentioned above, the majority of these interventions functioned by directly altering cellular transition probabilities during the simulation time steps. However, one intervention instead involved alteration of the Dinamica EGO allocation parameters, which was performed prior to the start of the simulations. The details of these spatial interventions are expansive and as such are presented in Appendix @sec-manipulation_details_app.

## Simulation evaluation {#sec-simulation_feedback}

Given that it is impossible to empirically validate future simulations [@vanvliet2016], we opted for two approaches to qualitatively evaluate the results. The first evaluation approach was to perform spatial analysis to identify patterns and trends in the simulation maps in order to compare them against the scenario narratives. Specifically, to evaluate landscape fragmentation, we calculated a selection of metrics using the R package *landscapemetrics* [@landscapemetrics] including: Effective Mesh Size [@jaeger2000]; Patch Cohesion Index [@schumaker1996] and Contagion Index [@riitters1996]. The former two metrics were calculated at both the landscape level (i.e. across all LULC classes) and at the level of individual LULC classes, with the latter only being possible to calculate at the landscape level.

In addition to this, to evaluate regional specific trends in LULCC contained in the scenario narratives, we also produced three summary metrics to capture changes in specific regions, namely: urban areas, mountainous areas, remote rural areas, and Protected Areas (PAs) (Details of how these regions were spatially identified are included in Appendix @sec-manipulation_details_app). The first summary metric, dubbed 'relative urban change', corresponded to the % area change in the Urban/amenities class between 2020-2060 in a given region relative to the change in the whole study area (to account for differences in overall change rates between scenarios). The second metric, 'change in urban patches', equated to the % difference in the number of patches of Urban/amenities class in the region in 2060 vs. 2020. Finally, 'change in natural LULC class cohesion' was calculated as the mean difference in the Patch Cohesion Index value between 2020-2060 in PAs for the natural/semi-natural LULC classes (Open Forest, Closed forest, Grassland or meadows, Overgrown/shrubland, Alpine pastures, and Intensive agriculture).

The second evaluation approach was to gather feedback on the simulation results from the participants who were involved in the scenario creation and other experts. To facilitate this, we created a webpage where invited participants could interactively view the simulated maps, in addition to info-graphics summarizing the LULCC trends, with the option to view multiple scenario outputs alongside each other. Participants were then provided the opportunity to provide qualitative feedback based on a series of open-ended questions framing key aspects of each scenario.

# Results and Discussion

## Scenario simulation overview {#sec-simulation_desc}

@fig-maps_plots shows the maps of simulated LULC in Switzerland in 2060 under all five scenarios although given the large scale of the study region readers are urged to explore the interactive versions of the maps using the web platform devised for feedback: <https://valpar.ch/land-use-change-scenarios/>.

\blandscape

![](figures_tables/P1_maps_pie_bars_lscape.png){fig-align="center" width="1200"}

![](figures_tables/P2_maps_pie_bars_lscape.png){fig-align="center" width="1200"}

![Maps of simulated LULC in Switzerland in 2060 under each scenario with accompanying pie charts the % coverage of each LULC class as well as bar charts showing the % difference in LULC class area between 2020 and 2060.](figures_tables/P3_maps_pie_bars_lscape.png){#fig-maps_plots fig-align="center" width="1200"}

```{=tex}
\newpage
\elandscape
```
The EI-NAT scenario is characterized by a relatively low rate of urban growth with a strong increase in the area of closed forest. The accompanying bar chart in @fig-maps_plots as well as the Sankey plot in @fig-sankey indicate that this increase in closed forest area is being driven primarily by the natural succession from open forest. However, the transitions from alpine pastures and overgrown/shrubland areas also represent substantial contributions. These trends, in combination with the reduction of land area utilized for intensive agriculture and permanent crops, collectively represent the loss of the open heterogeneous landscapes traditionally considered as culturally emblematic in Switzerland [@stotten2016]. This loss could result in a decline of perceived landscape quality amongst the population as such landscapes are highly valued under current conceptions [@wartmann2021; @mann2023]. However, this would theoretically be countermanded by the societal change towards a more nature-oriented perception of landscape quality proposed under the EI-NAT narrative. Of course, such a change in Switzerland is likely to be contentious and require societal negotiation [@bauer2009], as exemplified by the recent failure of an initiative to establish a new national park with strict nature-focused management [@michel2022].

Distinctive features of the EI-CUL scenario are that it exhibits the smallest loss of both alpine pastures and intensive agricultural land, as well as the greatest increase in grassland area. As described above, these LULC classes are the basis of the cultural landscape of Switzerland and hence their preservation (relative to other scenarios) is coherent with the normative focus of the EI-CUL scenario, i.e. maintaining the societal importance of agriculture. This is further exemplified by EI-CUL being the only scenario to present an increase (2.99%) in the area of permanent crops, of which a prominent example is vineyards, which are recognised as a characteristic cultural landscape in Switzerland [@rodewaldraimund2014].

The EI-SOC scenario displays the greatest loss of Overgrown/shrubland areas of all scenarios. This is in line with the scenario narrative which emphasizes spatial planning based on maximizing land use functionality and the provision of ecosystem services (referred to as Nature Contributions to People: NCPs). Under such a conception, overgrown areas would be perceived as offering little tangible benefits and hence there would likely be brought back into a more productive state in the form of managed grassland, pastures, or forest. This trend is reflected by the proportions of such transitions under this scenario in @fig-sankey.

The two exploratory scenarios, BAU and GR-EX, share many of the same defining characteristics, the foremost of which being a substantial increase in urban area (`r as.character(Scenario_area_chg_tbls[["BAU"]] %>%  filter(LULC_class == "Urban/amenities") %>%  select(clean_lab))` and `r as.character(Scenario_area_chg_tbls[["GR_EX"]] %>% filter(LULC_class == "Urban/amenities") %>%  select(clean_lab))` respectively, as compared to 2020) at the expense of a loss in area of almost all of the natural and semi-natural LULC classes. Closed forest is the only natural class to increase in area under both scenarios. Whereas grassland area does increase under GR-EX, @fig-sankey shows that this increase is driven by the abandonment of intensive agricultural land, and given additional time this grassland would likely also transition to forest. Perhaps the strongest difference between the exploratory scenarios is that BAU exhibits a higher increase in closed forest area because under GR-EX closed forest is converted to urban, intensive agriculture, and permanent crop areas, which are change that do not appear to occur under BAU. This, in combination with the fact that GR-EX displays a much greater rate of transitions across many classes (i.e. more instances of land use changes occurring in total, demonstrated in @fig-change_maps) is emblematic of the lack of cross-sectoral collaboration in spatial planning highlighted in the scenario narrative.

::: {#fig-sankey fig-align="center"}
![](figures_tables/Combined_sankey_start_end_without_persistence_portrait.png)

Sankey diagrams showing proportions of LULC class transitions between 2020 and 2060 under all scenarios\
*Note: for visual clarity instances of persistence, i.e. cells not undergoing change, were removed and transparency applied to lines representing transition quantities (coloured according to the initial LULC class).*
:::

It is also important to highlight common trends across the five scenarios and how these relate to similar studies. Firstly, all scenarios exhibit an increase in the area of the closed forests, which is to be expected given that none of the narratives specified a divergence from the current status-quo of a national policy of conservative forest management [@mather2000]. Indeed, this result is in concordance with previous LULCC studies in Switzerland [@gago-silva2017; @price2017; @gerecke2019] as well as Europe-wide studies [@rounsevell2006]. The loss of glacial area was also relatively high across all scenarios which was to be expected as our model incorporated results from glacial change predictions that indicate that glaciers are to lose between ca. 2/3 to almost the entirety of their volume by the end of the 21st century [@zekollari2019]. However, a limitation of our results is that we were unable to address the implications of this trend in terms of future LULC in the newly de-glaciated areas. The reason for this is that the predictions of glacial change incorporated in the LULCC-CA model only specified future glacier presence or absence and indeed the historical data of glacial transitions from the LULC maps contained too few instances to train robust statistical models to predict class to class transitions. Furthermore, the management of these areas would likely differ under each scenario, e.g through the prioritization of energy infrastructure [@farinotti2019] vs. creation of conservation areas [@bosson2023]. As such, additional research is needed to understand the potential for this newly accessible land.

## Realization of scenario specific spatial trends {#sec-spat_trends_results}

![Plots of differences in landscape metric values between scenarios at both the landscape and class scale: Patch cohesion index (landscape: A, class: B); Effective mesh size (landscape: C, class: D); and Contagion (E) index.](figures_tables/Combined_lsm_plots_portrait.png){#fig-LSMs fig-align="center"}

Focusing on the more detailed spatial trends described in the scenarios: both EI-SOC and EI-NAT contained statements related to the landscape being spatially segregated according to land use (i.e. settlement areas separated from agricultural areas) and the avoidance of landscape fragmentation (@tbl-Spat_ints) whereas, by contrast, GR-EX posits "uncoordinated" landscape management [@mayer2023]. To analyze whether these trends are evident in the simulation results, @fig-LSMs shows the values of three different landscape aggregation metrics: Patch Cohesion Index; Effective Mesh Size; and Contagion Index (higher values correspond to greater aggregation and hence lower fragmentation). @fig-LSMs shows that EI-NAT and EI-SOC do exhibit less landscape fragmentation than the EI-CUL and GR-EX scenarios, displaying the greatest values across all three metrics (@fig-LSMs: A, C, E). Vice versa, also in accordance with the scenario narrative, GR-EX exhibits the most pronounced landscape fragmentation (i.e. low metric values). The fact that BAU also presents low levels of landscape fragmentation, comparable to EI-NAT and EI-SOC, is somewhat surprising as it did not involve specific interventions intended to induce this effect as was the case in the normative scenarios. However, examining the class level values of the metrics, it is clear that the landscape level values for BAU are being driven primarily by high values for the Urban/amenities class in the case of the Patch Cohesion Index (@fig-LSMs B) and Closed forest for Effective Mesh Size (@fig-LSMs D). Although, the high BAU Patch Cohesion Index value for the Urban/amenities class is likely because this scenario exhibited substantially greater increase in the area of this class, as compared to the normative scenarios, hence it is more likely that patches of greater size would be formed. This highlights the need for caution when using landscape pattern metrics at the landscape scale to compare scenarios with different LULCC rates as the values may be biased due to strong differences in single classes.

\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.5}

```{r, message=FALSE, warning = FALSE}
#| label: tbl-regional_analysis
#| tbl-cap: "Measures of LULC change trends between 2020 and 2060, in spatial regions, across scenarios"

library(kableExtra)
#stop kableExtra automatically setting the output format of all kable objects to 'html' which means that markdown syntax is not rendered correctly
options(kableExtra.auto_format = FALSE)
kableExtra::kable(Region_tbl[,2:ncol(Region_tbl)], align=rep('c', ncol(Region_tbl)-1),
      booktabs = T,
      format = "latex"
      ,escape = TRUE
      ) |>
  kable_styling(full_width = FALSE)|>
  column_spec(1, width = "13em")|>
  kableExtra::group_rows(group_label = "Region", index = c(
    "Building zones" = sum(Region_tbl[["Region"]] == "Building zones"),
    "Rural municipalities" = sum(Region_tbl[["Region"]] == "Rural municipalities"),
    "Mountainous municipalities" = sum(Region_tbl[["Region"]] == "Mountainous municipalities"),
    "Protected areas"= sum(Region_tbl[["Region"]] == "Protected areas")),
    escape = TRUE,
    latex_gap_space = "0.1em")%>%
  footnote(general = "There are no spatial trend values for BAU and GR-EX under protected areas because neither scenario involved interventions in this region and hence the PA maps from the other scenarios were retrospectively applied for comparison.",
           symbol = c('% area change in Urban/amenities class between 2020-2060 in region  relative to the change in the entire study area;',
                      '% difference in the number of patches of Urban/amenities class in 2060 vs. 2020',
                      'Mean difference in cohesion index value between 2020-2060 in region for LULC classes: Open Forest, Closed forest, Grassland or meadows, Overgrown/shrubland, Alpine pastures, and Intensive agriculture'),
           threeparttable = TRUE,
           footnote_as_chunk = TRUE,
           symbol_title = "Symbols: ")
#threeparttable argument ensures footnote width corresponds to table width
```

\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1}

@tbl-regional_analysis presents several metrics (section @sec-simulation_feedback) to analyze scenario specific regionalized LULCC trends (section\@sec-manipulation). The three normative scenarios all contained the statement "No further urban sprawl" which is juxtaposed by the statement "Increase in buildings outside the construction zone, Industrial areas develop in an uncoordinated manner" in the GR-EX scenario. Whilst these statements are subjective, @tbl-regional_analysis shows that the interventions intended to operationalize them in the simulations had the intended effect as the normative scenarios show a substantially greater value of relative urban change inside the current designated building zones \citepalias{ARE_2022} compared to both the BAU and GR-EX scenarios.

Furthermore, EI-CUL specified a trend of "Revival of settlements in remote rural areas". Looking at the relative urban change value for rural municipalities alone would suggest that this does not appear to have been realized as EI-CUL exhibited the smallest increase in comparison to the other scenarios. However, the value of change in urban patches provides evidence to the contrary. Whilst all scenarios indicated a negative value of change in urban patches in rural municipalities (i.e. fewer patches in 2060 than in 2020), this is somewhat misleading given that some patches in the 2060 map will be new and others will represent the amalgamation of multiple patches from 2020. On this basis, the fact that EI-CUL exhibits the smallest (relative) decrease in patch number, combined with a value of urban area change, which is similar to the other scenarios, should correspond to a greater proportion of the remaining patches in 2060 being new patches. This would suggest that the scenario trend of revival of settlements in rural areas has been at least partly achieved despite the fact that some urban areas have disappeared. Similarly, EI-NAT and EI-SOC both included the statement: "Development of small towns in mountain areas". However, the values of relative urban change and change in urban patches in mountainous areas for these scenarios (@tbl-regional_analysis) imply that this trend has not been achieved as both sets of values are lower than for EI-CUL.

The trend of the expansion of PAs was implemented differently under each normative scenario according to the details in the narratives regarding their extent, siting and proposed management (Appendix @sec-PA_process). Thus, to identify differences between the normative scenarios, it is necessary to utilize comparisons to the BAU and GR-EX scenarios as neither involved changes in PAs. In this regard, @tbl-regional_analysis shows that the expansion of PAs under each of the normative scenarios led to substantially lower values of relative urban change, alongside higher values of change in natural LULC class cohesion in PAs as compared to the BAU and GR-EX scenarios. Both of these results are positive in terms of the normative priorities and goals for PA expansion expressed in the scenario narratives, namely: biodiversity protection (EI-NAT), NCP provision (EI-SOC) and cultural heritage protection (EI-CUL) (Appendix @sec-PA_process). The former result, equating to the restriction of urban area increase inside PAs, is desirable as the potential negative impacts of urbanization on biodiversity and NCPs is well-acknowledged [@urbaniza2013]. Vice versa, the corresponding increase in the connectivity of the natural and semi-natural LULC classes is typically beneficial for biodiversity and ecosystem services.

When comparing the scenarios, it is notable that the EI-SOC scenario resulted in considerably less relative urban change in PAs than the other normative scenarios (`r Region_tbl[Region_tbl$Region == "Protected areas" & Region_tbl$'Spatial trend in region' == "Relative % urban area change under scenario", "EI-SOC"]` vs. EI-NAT:`r Region_tbl[Region_tbl$Region == "Protected areas" & Region_tbl$'Spatial trend in region' == "Relative % urban area change under scenario", "EI-NAT"]`, EI-CUL:`r Region_tbl[Region_tbl$Region == "Protected areas" & Region_tbl$'Spatial trend in region' == "Relative % urban area change under scenario", "EI-CUL"]`). A possible explanation for this is that the PA areas chosen for EI-SOC are synergistic with other spatial interventions affecting urban development (e.g. the Urban migration intervention: @tbl-Spat_ints) or that they overlap with a greater proportion of land inherently unsuitable for urbanization as compared to the PAs under the other scenarios. If one of the intentions of establishing additional PAs, as part of Switzerland's EI, is indeed to limit the impact of urbanization, then this finding suggests that selecting sites by also considering their value for the provision of NCPs could lead to better results than focusing on biodiversity value alone.

## Expert participation and future directions {#sec-limitations}

The involvement of expert participation was essential for the formulation of the normative scenarios used in this research as these require the envisaging of system changes beyond just the extrapolation of trends required for exploratory scenarios. A prime example of this was the contribution of experts in devising the future LULC transition rates (Appendix @sec-trans_rates_app). This was very beneficial as the alternative would have been to estimate future rates from additional mechanistic or numerical models [@luo2022] which add additional complexity and intransparency to the modelling procedure and hence the risk of isolating stakeholders. Nonetheless, using expert elicitation to derive transition rates was challenging, principally due to the relatively large number of transitions to be modelled. This prompted the decision to reduce the scope to focusing on overall LULC class changes, however this created difficulties when it came to disaggregating these outputs back to rates for individual transitions. Similar difficulties in this process have been noted in other studies [@mallampalli2016; @karner2019] and in our case this was validated by the fact that several respondents in the subsequent expert feedback noted that they did not necessarily find the rates of change of certain LULC classes realistic.

A possible improvement to this process would be to use expert elicited values as a base point from which to sample a range of transition rate values and use these to perform repeated simulations under each scenario resulting in a spread, or ensemble, of future LULCC projections. This could be combined with permutations of other aspects of the model, such as the temporality and intensity of the spatial interventions, as well as the addition of other system 'shocks' (e.g extreme climatic events affecting land cover). Again, the choice of such permutations should be based on expert suggestions, i.e. identifying which changes are of most interest to investigate. In this sense, such an ensemble simulation approach could provide stakeholders with better insights into the plausibility of the scenarios but also the impacts of deliberate and uncontrolled changes to aspects of the system and the implications of these for the scenario outcomes [@uusitalo2015; @verkerk2018]. This would hopefully contribute to fostering an increased sense of stewardship as well as collaboration between actors which has been identified as being a prominent challenge to the implementation of EI in Switzerland [@wicki].

The expert feedback also highlighted that the multi-faceted nature of LULCC as a phenomenon makes it difficult to provide concrete statements as to the desirability of certain changes especially across different spatial scales. In this regard, we observed that even landscape metrics intended to summarize LULCC trends are difficult to interpret when comparing scenarios with different rates of change (section @sec-spat_trends_results). As such an additional aspect that could further improve the utility of the LULCC simulation results as a means to discuss the normativity of scenarios would be to represent the simulation results using more objective output measures. In the context of our study, given that the concept of EI is centered around the maintenance of biodiversity and ecosystem service provision, outputs encapsulating these would be a logical choice, especially as LULC is commonly a factor underpinning their quantification [@burkhard2009; @newbold2015; @gerecke2019; @gomes2021].

However, it is also important to note that the expert feedback did also result in generally positive comments. Respondents noted that the web platform was helpful for interacting with the results without the need for specialist software experience. Furthermore, one respondent from the Federal Office for the Environment (responsible for coordinating Switzerland's EI planning at the national level) stated that: "I think they *\[the simulation results\]* could be extremely useful as a basis for a dialogue on the desirability of different land use scenarios.".

# Conclusions

The importance of increasing the prevalence of explicitly normative future scenarios within the field of landscape research is clear as they create the ground to consider different worldviews and thus negotiate value-systems [@pascual2023] as well as explore solutions. The LULCC simulations of this study will hopefully highlight to decision-makers the opportunities, as well as potential conflicts, that may occur under the different scenarios of the development of a national Ecological Infrastructure in Switzerland. Beyond these results, this research has sought to demonstrate a replicable approach to operationalising and modeling normative scenarios of landscape change. While the modeling technique employed, constrained cellular automata, is not novel, the extent and depth to which the different aspects of scenarios were realized represents an innovative contribution to the field. Notably, these aspects included: iterative expert participation in deriving model inputs, the linkage of local and global scale phenomena, and the combination of machine learning and process-based models for multi-regional spatial prediction of LULCC. Furthermore, the fact that the model and data have been made publicly available allows others to build on the methodology in line with the suggested improvements. Namely: to include more opportunities for stakeholders to further shape the modelling process, thereby increasing ownership of the results and promoting stewardship.

# Acknowledgements {.unnumbered}

We would like to thank Olivier Broennimann (University of Lausanne) for assistance with the climatic data preparation, as well as the experts who provided feedback on the simulation results.

# Funding {.unnumbered}

We gratefully acknowledge financial support from the Swiss Federal Office for the Environment(FOEN) under the project "ValPar.CH: Values of the ecological infrastructure in Swiss parks" of the Action Plan of the Swiss Biodiversity Strategy.

\newpage

# Primary processes and inputs in the LULCC-CCA model {#sec-LULC_modelprocesses_app .appendix}

![Flowchart showing the primary processes and inputs in the LULCC-CCA model](figures_tables/Fig1.jpg){#fig-process fig-align="left" width="450"}

\newpage

# Details of land use and land cover aggregation {#sec-LULC_aggregation_app .appendix}

```{r, message=FALSE, warning = FALSE}
#| label: tbl-LULC_agg
#| tbl-cap: Aggregation of the Swiss area statistics land use land cover (LULC) classes.
#| tbl-colwidths: [50,50]
LULC_agg_summary <- LULC_agg %>%
  group_by(Aggregated_class_short) %>%
  summarise(test = toString(NOAS04_class_ENG)) %>%
  ungroup()
colnames(LULC_agg_summary) <- c("Aggregated LULC class", "Area statistics LULC classes")
#output as markdown table with Knitr::kable
knitr::kable(LULC_agg_summary, align=rep('c', ncol(LULC_agg_summary)), format = "markdown")
```

\newpage

# Land use and land cover transitions identified {#sec-transitions_app .appendix}

```{r, message=FALSE, warning = FALSE}
#| label: tbl-trans_details
#| tbl-cap: Land use land cover class-class transitions.
library(kableExtra)
#stop kableExtra automatically setting the output format of all kable objects to 'html' which means that markdown syntax is not rendered correctly
options(kableExtra.auto_format = FALSE)
#list file paths of transition datasets
Trans_dataset_names <- list.files(here("Data/Transition_datasets/Pre_predictor_filtering"),
                                  pattern = "regionalized",
                                  recursive = TRUE)
#create a table of details from the paths
Trans_datasets_tbl <- as.data.frame(str_split_i(Trans_dataset_names, "/", i = 1))
colnames(Trans_datasets_tbl) <- "Period"
Trans_datasets_tbl$Region <- str_split_i(Trans_dataset_names, "/", i = 2) |> str_split_i("\\.", i = 1)|> str_replace_all("_", " ")
Trans_datasets_tbl$From <- str_split_i(Trans_dataset_names, "/", i = 2) |> str_split_i("\\.", i = 2)
Trans_datasets_tbl$To <- str_split_i(Trans_dataset_names, "/", i = 2) |> str_split_i("\\.", i = 3) |> str_remove("_regionalized")
#clean LULC class names
Trans_datasets_tbl$From <- sapply(Trans_datasets_tbl$From, function(x){
  unique(LULC_agg[LULC_agg$Class_abbreviation == x, "Aggregated_class_short"])
})
Trans_datasets_tbl$To <- sapply(Trans_datasets_tbl$To, function(x){
  unique(LULC_agg[LULC_agg$Class_abbreviation == x, "Aggregated_class_short"])
})
#add dummy column
Trans_datasets_tbl$val <- "$\\checkmark$"
#pivot to wide table by date
Trans_tbl_wide <- tidyr::pivot_wider(data = Trans_datasets_tbl, names_from = c("Region"), values_from = "val")
#replace NAs with crosses
Trans_tbl_wide[is.na(Trans_tbl_wide)] <-  "$\\times$"
kable(Trans_tbl_wide[,2:ncol(Trans_tbl_wide)], align=rep('c', ncol(Trans_tbl_wide)-1),
      booktabs = T,
      format = "latex",
      escape = FALSE,
      col.names = c("Initial LULC class", "Final LULC class", paste(unique(Trans_datasets_tbl$Region)))) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = c("scale_down", "HOLD_position")) |>
group_rows(group_label = "Period", index = c(
  "1985-1997" = sum(Trans_tbl_wide$Period == "1985_1997"),
  "1997-2009" = sum(Trans_tbl_wide$Period == "1997_2009"),
  "2009-2018" = sum(Trans_tbl_wide$Period == "2009_2018")))
```

\newpage

# Details of glacier transition modeling {#sec-glacier_app .appendix}

@zekollari2019 used high-resolution regional climate model (RCM) simulations from the EURO-CORDEX ensemble [@jacob2014] to force a regional-scale glacier evolution model, resulting in 21st century projections of glacier changes over the entire European Alps. Here, results over all Swiss glaciers were extracted and reprojected from the original flowline approach (in glacier evolution model) to a distributed (map-view) representation of glacier geometry changes. This remapping was realized by ensuring a conservation of the area and volume of each elevation band. We evaluated these spatially distributed simulations of glacier changes at intervals of 5 years to match the simulation time steps, and aggregated them according to the RCP specific to each of the EI scenarios (see @tbl-scenario_operationalisation). Subsequently, the results were reprojected to the study domain by using a nearest neighbor scheme to determine whether a given location was glacier covered or ice free at each time step according to a given RCM. We then stratified these results by the respective RCP and computed the median glacier evolution. More specifically, we considered cells to be ice free at a given time step if 50% or more of the RCM simulations under the RCP predicted the glacier to have retreated from that location. For RCP 2.6, 4.5 and 8.5, this median evolution was based on a set of 9, 17 and 21 RCM simulations, respectively. \newpage

# Results of Cellular Automata allocation parameter calibration {#sec-parameter_calibration_app .appendix}

```{r, message=FALSE, warning = FALSE}
#| label: tbl-alloc_param_summary
#| tbl-cap: Summary statistics of the minimum similarity score values from the calibration of the LULCC-CCA allocation parameters.

library(knitr)

#load csv of allocation parameter summary statistics
Calibration_summary <- read.csv(here("publication/figures_tables/Validation_summary.csv"))

#replace . in column names with white space
colnames(Calibration_summary) <- str_replace_all(colnames(Calibration_summary), "\\.", " ")

#output as markdown table with Knitr::kable
knitr::kable(Calibration_summary, align=rep('c', ncol(LULC_agg_summary)), format = "markdown")
```

```{=tex}
\newpage
\blandscape
```
```{r, message=FALSE, warning = FALSE}
#| label: tbl-alloc_param_values
#| tbl-cap: Calibrated values of the transition allocation parameters, used in the simulations (Note some parameter values were deliberately altered under certain scenarios, see @tbl-Spat_ints).
#| tbl-colwidths: [20,20,12,12,12,12,12]

#load table of calibrated allocation parameter values
Alloc_params <- read.csv(here("publication/figures_tables/Optimal_param_table.csv"))

#replace '_' in column names with white space
colnames(Alloc_params) <- str_replace_all(colnames(Alloc_params), "_", " ")

#replace 'Perc' in column names with "percentage of columns with"
colnames(Alloc_params) <- str_replace_all(colnames(Alloc_params), "Perc", "% of transitions with")


#change all numeric values to 2 decimal places
Alloc_params[,2:ncol(Alloc_params)] <- lapply(Alloc_params[,2:ncol(Alloc_params)], function(x){
  round(x, digits = 2)
})

#get names of final two columns
perc_colnames <- colnames(Alloc_params)[(ncol(Alloc_params)-1):ncol(Alloc_params)]

#replace decimal values in these columns with percentages and % sign
Alloc_params[,perc_colnames] <- lapply(Alloc_params[,perc_colnames], function(x){
  paste0(x*100, "%")
})

#rename 'From.' and 'To.' columns to 'Initial LULC class' and 'Final LULC class'
colnames(Alloc_params)[1:2] <- c("Initial LULC class", "Final LULC class")

#match values in 'Initial LULC class' column to class names in LULC_agg
Alloc_params[,1:2] <- lapply(Alloc_params[,1:2], function(x){
  sapply(x, function(y){unique(LULC_agg[LULC_agg$Aggregated_ID == y, "Aggregated_class_short"])})
})

#output as markdown table with Knitr::kable
knitr::kable(Alloc_params, align=rep('c', ncol(LULC_agg_summary)),
      format = "markdown")
```

```{=tex}
\elandscape
\newpage
```
\newpage

# Details of preparation of dynamic predictor data for simulations {#sec-Sim_data_prep_app .appendix}

For the variables of average annual change in full time equivalents per labor market region in each economic sector (primary, secondary and tertiary), we used the future economic sector projections produced by @cretegny2020. This data is presented at an annual level and thus it was aggregated to average annual change for each simulation time point based on the preceding 5 years, i.e. for 2030, average annual change was calculated between 2025-2030.

Future climatic data utilized the same source as the historic data [@broennimann2023a; @broennimann2023b] with an adaptation of the CHclim25 downscaling methodology (*ibid*) in order to produce data aligning with the simulation time points. This approach used 2 km grid resolution datasets from the CH2018 Swiss climate projections for the period 1981-2099 [@fischer2022], as well as 1 km resolution daily MeteoSwiss grid data products for the reference period 1981-2010 \citepalias{meteoswiss2021}. These products include daily mean, maximum and minimum temperatures and daily precipitation sums. For the historical reference period 1981-2010, variable-specific downscaling methods are applied to the MeteoSwiss datasets. Temperature data are subjected to a local linear regression analysis to model the relationship between elevation and temperature. The derived coefficients are then disaggregated to a 25m grid resolution and spatially smoothed. In contrast, precipitation data are directly resampled to 25m resolution and smoothed. For the future time steps of climatic variable projections (2020 to 2060 in five-year increments). Anomalies were first computed as the deviation between the average conditions of each future period and the 1981-2010 baseline average, using only the CH2018 data. These anomalies were then resampled to 25m and summed with the downscaled 1981-2010 MeteoSwiss datasets to generate high-resolution future projections. Finally, the average number of growing degree days (GDDs) were calculated for specified temperature baselines of 0°C, 3°C, and 5°C. These calculations consisted of summing the daily average temperatures per year that exceeded the respective baselines for each of the future simulation time periods.

The variable of population per municipality was produced from the Swiss future population growth scenarios 2020-2050 \citepalias{swissfederalofficeforstatistics2020}. However, this data is non-spatial and only produced at the cantonal level and as such it was necessary to incorporate a process to make extrapolations at the municipal level. This involved fitting linear regression models for each canton of the relationship between the % of the cantonal population per municipality (dependent variable) and the % of cantonal urban area per municipality using data from the final year of the calibration period (2018). These models were then used to iteratively predict the % of cantonal population for each municipality in each simulation time step using the municipal % of cantonal urban area calculated from the LULC map of the preceding time step. The predicted % of cantonal population values were then used to divide the figure of cantonal population to give an estimated value of the municipal population.

\newpage

# Preparation of scenario specific transition rates {#sec-trans_rates_app .appendix}

Initially, transition rates were calculated for each of the calibration time periods before being extrapolated (linear models) over 5-year time steps between 2020 and 2060. These extrapolated transitions rates were dubbed the 'reference' rates and were applied to the values of LULC class extents in the final calibration year (2018) to estimate reference class areas for 2060.

These reference class areas were used as a prompt in discussions with experts involved in the scenario creation process to code whether statements within the scenarios were perceived as likely to lead to an increase or decrease in the areas of certain LULC classes relative to the reference areas. Experts were encouraged to focus only on the direct impacts of statements and not potential indirect impacts, i.e. statements referencing change in agriculture practices were linked to agricultural LULC classes only and the potential for subsequent impacts on forestry were only considered in corresponding statements. Importantly, experts were able to allocate different magnitudes of impact on LULC classes due to individual statements. This was realized in a simplistic manner through the allocation of a desired number of either pluses and minuses, each representing an equally weighted percentage change to the class area, which were summed for each class across all statements to give overall percentage impacts of the scenario on class areas. This process was first employed for the BAU scenario with the percentage class impacts used to modify the reference 2060 class areas to give projected 2060 class areas. This was then repeated for the remaining scenarios (EI-NAT, EI-CUL, EI-SOC and GR-EX); however, their areas were derived relative to the BAU rather than the reference areas. This is an important distinction as the scenarios are intended to represent deliberate changes to the status-quo represented by the BAU narrative.

Finally, it was necessary to dis-aggregate the projected class areas for each scenario back to areas and then rates for each transition. Given that it is possible to either increase or decrease the rates of transitions that result in the expansion of LULC class area simultaneously to altering transitions that result in class area contraction, this process of dis-aggregation represents an optimization problem. Our method to address this was to perform a first pass over the LULC classes and allocate areas to relevant transitions with respect to the proportion of area expansion vs. contraction exhibited by the LULC class in the calibration periods. In the case that there were multiple transitions contributing to either class expansion or contraction then the values were ascribed based on the relative contributions of each transition to historic class change rates. This gave us an approximation of the transition areal changes for each scenario across all time steps with further manual changes made in order to reduce the discrepancy between the sum of these and the expert projected class areas for 2060.

\newpage

# Details of manipulation of transition potential and allocation parameters {#sec-manipulation_details_app .appendix}

@tbl-Spat_ints contains statements regarding spatial changes processes in each of the scenarios and details the mechanisms by which these were instantiated as spatial interventions in the simulation modeling. The interventions surrounding the expansion of PAs were particularly detailed and hence detailed explanations are provided in the subsequent section.

## Identifying areas for protected area expansion {#sec-PA_process}

First, we identified which categories of Protected Areas (PAs) in Switzerland were included in the calculation of national PA coverage estimates. Then we combined aggregated geodata for these PA categories [@rey_2023] with data of Cantonal PAs provided directly by the Swiss Federal Office for the Environment (FOEN) and data of biodiversity promotion areas on Agricultural land (reference). In the process we removed any overlap between the data sources.

Calculating the additional PA areal coverage required to meet the targets in the scenarios requires an estimate of current PA coverage. Given that published estimates of PA coverage in Switzerland differ we opted to calculate a value directly from the spatial data. We trialed methods of areal calculation using both raster and vector data sources with the latter producing a figure closer to published estimates. Using the current coverage we calculated the additional coverage area required to meet the targets under each scenario as follows: EI-SOC 4.13%, EI-CUL 7.13% and EI-NAT 12.13%.

To select the locations to fulfill the additional area requirements we used modifications of the same approach for each of the 3 normative scenarios. This employed a spatial conservation prioritization map produced with the software Zonation [@lehtomäki2013] with different selections of patches used for the EI-SOC scenario vs. the EI-NAT and EI-CUL according to their respective objectives (@tbl-Spat_ints). The EI-NAT scenarios states that PA expansion should prioritize "species and habitats important for biodiversity, ecosystem processes and functions"[@mayer2023]. As such we utilized a prioritization map produced from maps of the predicted occurrence for 7040 species in Switzerland produced by researchers at the University of Lausanne using a state of the art species distribution modeling pipeline: N-SDM [@adde2023b]. First, we excluded existing settlement areas, roads and railways according to the SwissTLM dataset \citepalias{swissfederalofficeoftopography2011}. Then we identified patches of high priority values in the map using the 'get_patches' function of the *landscapemetrics* R package [@landscapemetrics], and simultaneously calculating the median of cell priority values for each patch. As part of this process we removed patches that consisted of two raster cells or less (approx. 2 hectare) as it was reasoned that new PAs would be unlikely to be designated at this size. Following this, we used a solver function to identify possible combinations of patches whose combined total area equated to the additional area of PAs required under the scenario. This function was specified to return an upper limit of 10,000 solutions although in each case this was not exceeded. We then identified the set of patches that gave the highest priority by ranking all of the solutions according to their values of average median patch priority. Given that the scenarios specify that PA expansion occurs over time it was necessary to separate the final set of priority patches into separate maps to be used in each simulation time step. This was done by first ranking the patches according to highest median priority values and then dividing the number of patches by the number of simulation time steps to give an index of patches to be added in each step. This meant that the 2020 map started with only the existing PAs with each subsequent map containing all of the PAs of the preceding time steps plus the new patches to be added in that step.

The EI-CUL scenario utilized the same prioritization map as EI-NAT however the patches were first filtered on the basis of a minimum distance to an area of the federal inventory of landscapes and natural monuments of natural importance (BLN) \citepalias{foen_2017}. Whereas the EI-SOC scenario the spatial prioritization map was instead produced using modeled spatial data for the provision of 7 Nature's Contributions to People (NCPs: otherwise referred to as ecosystem services) produced by the the University of Geneva [@külling2023]. These included: nutrient retention; recreation; landscape suitability for pollination; pest control; hazard regulation; carbon storage in vegetation; air quality regulation. Otherwise the process of ranking patches and separating the PA maps for the simulation time points under EI-CUL and EI-SOC followed the same procedure as the EI-NAT scenario albeit using the different area expansion targets.

```{=tex}
\newpage
\blandscape
```
```{r, message=FALSE, warning = FALSE}
#| label: tbl-Spat_ints
#| tbl-cap: Details of interventions to spatially manipulate cellular transition proabilities
#| tbl-colwidths: [5,20,20,10,10,10,25]
library(knitr)
#replace '_' in scenario_names
Spat_ints$Scenario <- str_replace_all(string = Spat_ints$Scenario, pattern = "_", "-")
#replace '_' in Intervention IDs
Spat_ints$Intervention_ID <- str_replace_all(string = Spat_ints$Intervention_ID, pattern = "_", " ")
#convert LULC class names to character vectors
# Spat_ints$LULC_class_target <- sapply(Spat_ints$LULC_class_target, function(x) {
#     x <- str_remove_all(x, " ")
#     rep <- unlist(strsplit(x, ","))
#     },simplify=FALSE)
#
# #clean LULC class names
# Spat_ints$test <- sapply(Spat_ints$LULC_class_target, function(x){
#   test <- unique(LULC_agg[which(LULC_agg$Class_abbreviation %in% x), "Aggregated_class_short"])
#   browser()
#
#   unique(LULC_agg[LULC_agg$Class_abbreviation == x, "Aggregated_class_short"])
# })
#clean column names
colnames(Spat_ints) <- str_replace_all(string = colnames(Spat_ints), pattern = "_", " ")
#output as markdown table with Knitr::kable
kable(Spat_ints, align=rep('c', ncol(Spat_ints)), format = "markdown") |> kable_styling(font_size = 4)
```

```{=tex}
\elandscape
\newpage
```
